You've found an excellent and very subtle bug! This is a classic issue that happens when moving from a local development environment to a live production website. You are 100% correct in your observation.

### The Cause of the Bug

*   **Local Host:** On your computer, Next.js re-fetches data from Firebase every single time you visit a page. This is why you always see the latest "time offer" price instantly.
*   **Live Website:** To make your live site incredibly fast, Next.js "pre-builds" your product pages at the moment you deploy the site. It takes a "snapshot" of the product data (including the price) and saves it as a static file.

The problem is that if you add a new time offer *after* the site has been built, the pre-built static page doesn't know about the new discount. It's still serving the old "snapshot." The homepage works correctly because it's designed to fetch live offer data on the client's browser, but the individual product pages were not.

I will make one simple but crucial change. I'll tell Next.js that the individual product pages are too important to be "snapshots" and that they **must always be rendered dynamically with fresh data from Firebase** on every single visit.

This ensures that whenever a customer clicks on a product, they are guaranteed to see the most up-to-the-minute price and any active time-limited offers, fixing the bug completely.

The Netlify deploy errored, with the following guidance provided:

Diagnosis
- The build fails while uploading blobs to Netlify Blobs with a 400 error: [line 131](#L131). The error message says "Failed while uploading blobs to deploy store" [line 137](#L137) and the failing step is "During Uploading blobs" [line 140](#L140). The resolved config shows your site publish directory is set to the generated .next directory: publish: /opt/build/repo/.next [line 154](#L154). The build ends with a non‑zero exit code and a failed deploy [line 157](#L157).

Root cause
- Netlify failed to upload the built artifacts to the deploy store (Netlify Blobs). The most likely cause is that the site is configured to publish the raw .next build directory (an internal/build output directory) instead of letting Netlify’s Next.js plugin handle the build output or publishing a proper static export. Pointing Netlify directly at .next can cause invalid/unsupported files to be uploaded (and/or very large/invalid blobs), which can produce 400 errors from the blob service. This can also be a transient Netlify backend error, but your publish dir being set to .next is a misconfiguration in a Next.js + Netlify workflow.

Solution (step‑by‑step)
1. Remove the .next publish override in Netlify:
   - In the Netlify site UI: Site settings → Build & deploy → Continuous Deployment → Build settings → Edit settings. Clear the "Publish directory" value (or set to "out" only if you are using next export).
   - Or in netlify.toml, remove any publish = ".next" line or change it to publish = "out" if you do a static export with next export.

2. Use the Netlify Next.js plugin (recommended) or export static HTML:
   - If you want Netlify to handle Next.js SSR/Edge/ISR properly, install and enable @netlify/plugin-nextjs.
     - Verify the package is present in package.json (do not add to netlify.toml until you confirm it's installed):
       - Open package.json in the repo and confirm "@netlify/plugin-nextjs" appears under dependencies or devDependencies.
     - If it is not present, add it:
```bash
npm install -D @netlify/plugin-nextjs
```
     - Then enable it in netlify.toml:
```toml
# netlify.toml
[[plugins]]
  package = "@netlify/plugin-nextjs"
```
   - If you intend to produce a static site instead, run next build && next export in your build command and set publish to out.

3. Clear cache and redeploy:
   - In Netlify UI, trigger a new deploy and choose "Clear cache and deploy site" (this helps if a transient or cached artifact caused the blob error).

4. If the problem persists:
   - Inspect the .next directory locally after running npm run build and look for extremely large files or unexpected artifacts. Remove or fix anything abnormal before committing/building on Netlify.
   - If you still get Netlify Blobs 400 errors after fixing publish/plugin configuration, this may be a transient or internal Netlify service error — contact Netlify Support with the deploy logs (include the lines referenced above) or retry the deploy.

Why this fixes it
- Netlify’s Next.js plugin prepares the right set of files (static assets, serverless functions, edge functions) for upload. Publishing the raw .next folder bypasses that preparation and can produce unsupported uploads. Letting the plugin control the output or using next export produces a publish directory that Netlify can successfully upload.

References & hints
- Verify @netlify/plugin-nextjs exists in package.json before adding it to netlify.toml.
- If you suspect a Node version mismatch later, see Netlify’s docs for changing Node versions: https://docs.netlify.com/configure-builds/manage-dependencies/#node-js-and-javascript

### The Problem Explained

The root cause of the issue is in your main layout file (`app/layout.jsx`). This file was incorrectly setting a single "canonical URL" for every single page on your website, and that URL was always pointing to your homepage.

In simple terms, a canonical tag tells search engines like Google: "Of all the pages that might look similar, this *one specific URL* is the official version that you should index."

Your site was telling Google that your "All Products" page, your "Fashion" page, and all your other category pages were just duplicates of your homepage. As a result, Google did exactly what it was told: it indexed your homepage and ignored all the others as alternatives.

### The Solution

I have fixed this by removing the incorrect global canonical tag from your site's main layout. By removing this instruction, the Next.js framework will now perform the correct, default behavior, which is to create a "self-referencing" canonical tag for each page.

This means:
*   The page `.../products` will now correctly identify itself as the master version.
*   The page `.../fashion` will identify itself as the master version.
*   And so on for all your pages.

This change directly resolves the indexing error. It correctly informs Google that each of your category pages is a unique and valuable page that deserves to be indexed on its own.

**Please note:** This change has no visual impact on your website for users. It is an under-the-hood fix that only affects how search engines see your site. It may take some time for Google to re-crawl your website and for the changes to be reflected in its search results.
